what_it_does: Catalogues roughly 150 design rules that the source design language contains but the rendered output misses or misrepresents, reframed as RENDER gaps and tagged with markers such as [NEW], [not-rendered], [WRONG], and [unchecked].
observations: The ledger is explicitly a corrected lens: earlier synthesis asked whether tokens exist in face JSON, while this file asks whether the rendered screen matches intent.

It is organized into nine thematic sections: gradients/shading/opacity, type hierarchy, padding/alignment/spacing, components, palette, geometry, layout/zones, sequence/attention-flow, and grounds/texture.
Each finding is tagged with one of [NEW], [not-rendered], [WRONG], or [unchecked] to indicate its relationship to the live render.
Some items include empirical measurements, e.g., device well luminance 245.6, gold value #E3C421 vs source-true #C8A800.
It introduces a large set of named components and atoms, including pill, pill_row, tag, glass_panel, status_dot, badge_pair, stat_orb, flow_pipeline, scope_box, and zone_divider.
It records narrative and attention-flow rules such as the one-idea-per-slide law, depth-compression law, data-slide funnel, and two-move opening/matched close.
It ends with an honesty disclaimer: the list is not guaranteed exhaustive and the ideal validation is an adversarial render audit against the actual screen.
No code symbols are present; the file is pure design-system documentation.
standouts: The deliberate shift from token-existence coverage to rendered-result fidelity as the audit criterion.
The depth-compression law and audience-keyed arc variants, which frame design grammar as scale-invariant and occasion-specific.
The large [NEW] ACTION ATOM layer, which the print deck never had.
The rule that gold is the sole attention currency with a computed budget of 3-5 gold instances per view.
conventions: Uses Markdown headings and bullet lists.
Tags each finding with a bracketed status marker.
Groups findings into alphabetized thematic sections.
Mixes empirical measurements, named tokens, and high-level principles in the same list.
Includes a dated correction note and an honesty disclaimer.
concerns: Many items are tagged [unchecked] or [NEW] with no linked sample, measurement, or reproduction path.
The ledger relies heavily on a single observer's eye and named authority without explaining methodology.
Some rules appear to conflict or are described as unresolved, e.g., octagon vs hexagon entity assignment and solid-vs-dashed meaning flips.
It introduces many new components and laws without showing where they originate in the source.
notes: A qualitative, screen-level gap ledger that reframes design-system coverage as rendered-output fidelity failures across visual grammar, layout, and narrative flow.
questions: What is the exact SOURCE artifact and what is the RENDER pipeline being audited?
Who is Tim and what is the FACE-JSON format referenced in the corrected lens?
How were the live-render measurements taken, and on which devices or viewports?
What is the C1 test mentioned for the decision-surface option-label and option-implication slots?
How will [NEW], [not-rendered], [WRONG], and [unchecked] items be prioritized or verified?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to re-lens earlier synthesis findings as render gaps, and the file does that; it also introduces new rules and leaves many items unchecked, so it functions partly as an audit log and partly as a growing rule set.
apparent_themes: Design grammar as a formal, enumerable system
Attention economics and scarcity of emphasis
Narrative sequence and scale-invariant depth compression
intention_signals: To create a screen-grounded audit vocabulary that holds rendering code accountable to visual and narrative design intent rather than merely checking that tokens exist.
novelty: It reframes design-system completeness as a set of rendered-result gaps, with explicit status markers and a depth-compression law for adapting grammar across 1/15/30-page occasions.
contribution: Provides a categorized, marker-annotated inventory of visual, typographic, spatial, and narrative rules that a fused system can use to validate whether rendered output matches the source design intent.
summary_for_embedding: A markdown gap ledger listing approximately 150 design-system rules that are missing, mis-rendered, or newly identified in rendered output, organized into categories such as gradients, typography, spacing, components, palette, geometry, layout, sequence, and grounds.
intention_for_embedding: To record a render-level audit of design intent versus actual screen output for a counterpart design system.
